sentences.text: Elizabeth Parker.
Spitzer, Elizabeth Spitzer.
In Hungary, Budapest.
1924, The 25 of March.
Thank you.
Yes.
My grandfather was a non-Jew.
He became to be a Jew because my grandmother was a beautiful woman.
And he was a very nice man and loved his family.
And my mother was a beautiful woman for-- I think it's every child that the mother is the beautifulest woman in the world.
And she got married with my father under the first war.
And it wasn't a happy marriage.
I was the third child.
And I was six years old when they divorced.
Then we have ups and downs, but when I was a teenager I have a lovely life.
I go to the school.
I got-- I mix all the children.
Was Jewish-- we never had anything between us, who is Jewish and who is not Jewish.
And when I was 16 in high school, then-- I meet a lovely boy.
We have our school sweethearts.
But he wasn't a Jew, again.
And later on, when Hitler come in, his mother come to talk to my mother to leave him alone.
I don't want to do anything with him, but that was my first heart broke-- because I am a Jew.
We lived with my grandmother, my mother's mother.
And we had a big house.
We had 24 tenants.
And we have a big garden, and all the children-- we have sometimes 20 children in my house, because it was a big house.
And all around the neighbors would come over and play with us and we were very happy.
And then, when I went to school, it started a different way of life.
I have one brother whom I don't know.
He died when he was three.
This was before my time.
He has chicken pox, and the doctor gave a wrong instruction to my mother, told her to give him cold.
Should give him a hot bath.
So he died, and my mother was very heartbroken.
And then I come to the world.
My brother was two years older than me.
His name was Paul.
He was a very lovely boy.
He was blue eyes, brown curly hair.
It's a beautiful boy and a very good nature.
And we lived with my mother.
And my mother was a very sensitive and very fine lady.
She played the piano beautifully.
She taught us all the operettes and opera.
And she loved to make tapestry.
We sit with her with my brother and we fixed the needles for her and she was so-- so-- so quick in the hand-- in her hand.
And both so fast-- my brother and me fixed the needles to all the different colored cottons because she made tapestry.
And-- but she was very unhappy.
Only the-- everything was two-- her two-- her two children.
She never got married.
She always said, my children have a father, so she don't want to hear about it.
My father was a different type of man.
He was more selfish-- I-- in my eyes.
And he loved-- he loved us in his way, but it was not good enough for me, and not it was not good enough for my brother, either.
So I was not a very happy child.
And-- but how I grew older, and I get 15, 16, you know, then it's-- started to have different interests, not only the father, not only the mother, you know?
But my mother always was good to us, and always understand my feelings, and always calmed me down.
And my father's second marriage, he has another son.
And he was a lovely little boy.
And my mother always said, you have to love him because he can't help him coming in that world.
Even-- we have a summer house in [NON-ENGLISH], and even we take him to have a summer holiday.
Even my mother never talked to my father, but we take the little boy because she was-- my mother was a very good-hearted woman, and she feels sorry for that little boy.
So we take him for a holiday.
His second wife, my mother never talked to her, because she fix her marriage, you know.
But for the little boy, he-- my mother always said, you have to love him.
He's your brother.
And that's how we were brought up.
I can't say, I don't think so my step-mother, I should call her, she said the same things to my brother.
[LAUGHS] But she was a different type of woman than my mother was.
No, because was no man in the house.
My-- we go every [NON-ENGLISH] we go to the synagogue.
And when I was a teenager, every Friday night I go to the synagogue with my friends.
And in Hungary, in our town, every Saturday afternoon was our junior [NON-ENGLISH]-- how you call it?
Service.
And we meet everybody in the Jewish public school ground and we go to the synagogue.
Every Saturday afternoon we have to go.
And the rabbi always spoke to us nicely.
His name was Dr.
Friedman Danesh.
He was a very, very clever and nice man.
And he looks like Jesus Christ when they're painting Jesus, you know?
He was-- he was a beautiful man.
He went to Auschwitz with his family, too.
And your own family, we they a traditional Jewish family?
Yes.
We was not a-- how you call i?
Not a religious, I mean, we were an [NON-ENGLISH] Jew.
Modern Jew.
And then we have our different synagogue, a beautiful synagogue, like here in the central synagogue.
And the religious one have the-- the pious and the things, you know, they have separately, their synagogue.
Oh, yes, because every Saturday afternoon we have a party, how you call it?
Because we meet each-- that's how I meet my husband because he was in the same synagogue he belongs to.
And even when we was 10, 12 years old, he always look at me, [LAUGHS] and I always look at him.
But when we were finished, we go in different way.
He went different way.
Yes, we had a beautiful holiday house.
My grandmother had a hillside, and she built her three daughters in one ground, one big ground, three different summer houses.
So when I was two years old, she built it, for me-- always said to me, because I was the youngest daughter, youngest grandchild.
And she loved me very much.
And that was so beautiful place.
My uncle was a very rich man and they were always going to Switzerland and Italy to holiday.
But he said, that was-- that [NON-ENGLISH] was as beautifulest.
No, it's two hours in the train, in the slow train.
And now it's only one hour, but I still have it.
It's-- it was a lovely, lovely place.
My father was a-- how do you call it in English?
Not a courier, he has about the 16 pairs of horses, and eight race horses, and four big trucks, and four taxis.
You know, he was a--
Not a courier.
An expeditor, maybe, you call it.
Expeditor no?
I don't know how you call it in English but he was a clever man--
Yes, yes, yes, yes.
Yeah, eight.
Unfortunately, yes.
He liked it very much.
We had our stalls in the [NON-ENGLISH] in [NON-ENGLISH], where he keep his horses.
And every-- every weekend we had to see him because that was the law.
And he put us in the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the horse carriage.
And we went to the stalls to see the horses.
No, I hated it because it made me miserable in my life because he spends so much money.
No, only horses.
Oh, yes.
Even when I was in the ghetto, I was so upset because he said to me, if you pass that way could you take that bet for me, and he-- I said, Daddy-- how can you?
You son is in the [NON-ENGLISH] camp, I am in the ghetto.
And do you have heart to spend some money to the horses?
I hate that.
He said it's none of your business, none of your business.
That's-- it was not my business, but I always say what I-- how I feel.
That's my trouble.
[LAUGHS]
It was a happy time.
I went to kindergarten and then I went to the public school.
And then I went to the high school.
And then I went to the finishing school where I learned to be a-- [NON-ENGLISH], a teacher of the hand sewing, you know?
But then come Hitler, and I can't finish my studying.
So I only finished when I was 17.
And I learned how to cut, make patterns, designing, styling.
And I was very, very grateful to my mother to teach me because my mother went to the same school when she was a young girl.
And all her sisters, the three of them, they go.
And my cousins go to the same school.
And it was a very, very happy life, till Hitler comes.
I loved singing and I was the youngest in the choir-- in the school choir.
I was, I think, at that time nine or 10.
But I played tennis and I ride the bike-- the bike, we loved to ride the bike.
That time it was such a big thing, you know, to somebody have a bike.
But my mother tried to provide everything what she can.
Provide, yeah.
In that way, yes.
Only I was heartbroken because I have no real family life and I miss it and even when I get married I always said that's what I want, that's what I miss.
Even now when I see my little granddaughter who is 13 now, and she sits in my son's lap that I always say, that's what I miss in my life.
I haven't got that.
Only go for a lunch, and then I go home because I can't-- I can't stand that atmosphere, you know?
Because his wife don't think much about me and always was pushy and I can't take it.
How I said to you, I always said what I feel, and I even told her-- she leave me alone, because you break my life and--
Oh, yes.
And even now we're still friends.
And I have Jewish friends and I have non-Jewish friends.
But how I say it, that girl who was very close to me, Eva, I still go to Hungary because of her.
And I still help her any way how I can and I always help her.
No, but when my mother was in the ghetto, her mother-- her father was very high in the miniserium.
And he's-- her mother take my mother in the ghetto a piece of bread, because she knows she's alone and she hasn't got anything to eat.
Of course, you know, but it was a funny thing.
We went to shopping always in one store with my non-Jewish girlfriend.
And one day, the boss said to me, come, I want to talk to you.
I said, what do you want?
He said, why do you have a friend a Jewish girl like her?
I said, you stupid!
I am the Jewish one, not she.
And he was so, so upset.
And we go every Saturday afternoon, we have school dance.
And my brother, who learned a textile industry, he going in the technical college and his friends coming to the same dancing school where we go.
So everybody know him, he was a Jewish boy.
And when I don't like somebody, I beckon my brother and he takes me off of his hands.
And two or three times he comes with the same boy and then the boy said to me, if once more the Jewish boy comes here, I kick him.
I said, you know who is that Jewish boy?
That's my brother.
And get!
[LAUGHS] I tell him off.
He was so upset.
[LAUGHS] He never dreamed he was my brother.
So it's the little things.
And, you know, my girlfriends-- one whose father was a school-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Headmaster of the school.
And we embroidered and listened to the radio.
And then one day-- Sunday afternoons-- and one day we were sitting and then the Hungarian soldiers come in, because the Jewish people can't have radio.
So he comes in, take off the radio.
And we was-- we was-- just looking.
No voice coming out of our mouths and no, that-- three of my girlfriends who were not Jewish then but they were in my place.
We was crying, and we can't say a word.
They take our radio away.
It was only one thing that was to us an afternoon, and then they take it away.
Yeah, they said that's unbelievable.
But what can they do?
What they can do?
A 17, 16-year-old girl?
What she can do?
Nothing.
They went home and tell their parents what has happened.
His second wife was born non-Jew.
Then he she married and she became-- became to be a Jew.
And she has two children.
And when she married my father, like a Jewish woman.
And then, when Hitler started to come, and they have their little boy, and they're going back to her religious.
So my father leave the Jewish religious, and go with her, in the Catholic.
So she originally wasn't Jewish, she married a Jew,
And then she--
Yes
Yeah
Yes.
Yes, again.
And they had Catholic papers at this stage.
Yes, yes, yes.
I tell you honestly, I-- I-- I can't say a word because it was his life.
But I was hurt, because then I feel it, again-- he leave us down.
We was not her family anymore-- he has his new family, the new religious-- and that's how-- that's how I feel.

And I think we go through that out of each other there because he don't come to the synagogue anymore, and we have-- my mother-- my mother's mother bought us a seat-- you know how it is in a synagogue, you have to buy a seat.
And then because he left the Jewish fate, they take away the seat what my mother's mother bought.
And my brother, it was just before bar mitzvah.
And my mother said, how you can take it away?
Here is the boy who is a bar mitzvah and he needs the seat.
And they take it away.
So, again, we were hurt because of my father.
So all my life he was not so, how I say it to you, not so close.
Did he support the family financially or he didn't need to?
He has to because the law tell him, but he didn't.
And he-- first of all, he don't want to divorce my mother.
It's always said to my mother, you are my wife, she is only my girlfriend.
And my mother said, I don't want to eat a cherry in the same plate with anybody.
So that was the two different type of characters-- my mother and my father.
But he did divorce her?
Of course.
But my grandmother always said to me-- to us-- cost us a fortune.
One house went to the lawyers.
Because for two years, he don't want to divorce.
But my grandmother let my mother swear she will never go back to him because of his nature.
But my mother loved him.
Still, when we went to the ghetto, my father sent the courier, his trucks and things to take the furnishings and everything.
And after that many years, I see my mother and father together.
I never see them together.
And that was my heartbreak, you know?
That breaks my heart.
And all my life I carry on with me that heartbreak.
Elizabeth, let's go to 1942.
You finished school--
Yes.
Yes.
I worked in Budapest, the very high couture, we make only the film stars and things.
And I was the one who will making [INAUDIBLE], her-- the lady-- the film star's frocks, make it in and take it out.
You know?
And I was around them.
And--
About 35 minutes by train, by [NON-ENGLISH]--
Tram, yeah.
And I go, but it was a very, very high couture.
In [NON-ENGLISH].
And the name was Vogue.
Oh, yes.
[NON-ENGLISH], you know?
And they like me.
They always said, what a nice little girl.
Because I was always around them and pleased them.
And they liked me.
After after when-- when we have to go to the ghetto then I can't do in the tram because then you have to wear the yellow star, you can't go in the train, and I can't walk for-- that far.
And then they were so clever when the Germans are coming in March 1944 in Hungary, my brother was not home anymore.
He was in the [NON-ENGLISH] camp.
[NON-ENGLISH] camp.
And then they-- when they took us to the ghetto, then slowly and slowly the young girls have to go to the-- to the German military factories.
So then in the morning, because I work in shift work, so I find a job, because we have to support us.
We have no money anymore.
Oh, yes.
They sent us a paper.
1944, in end of March, April.
And they said in two weeks time you have to leave your home, your house, and you have to go to the-- that and that address.
You have one room with my-- with your family.
So we went with my-- my mother's brother and his family.
We went together in one little flat.
After the March 19, when the Germans are coming.
Which shop?
Yes, I was working there.
No.
No, no.
Nobody knows I am Jewish or not Jewish, nobody is asking me, Only when the Germans are coming.
In [NON-ENGLISH].
Of course, we were listening to the radio, even if they take that radio away.
But we have another radio and we're listening in night time.
Yes, you know.
